channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: The AI Tutor That Makes Kids Actually Think | E2298
gist: Brilliant CEO Sue Kim explains why the company's just-launched Socratic AI tutor 'Cooji' rests on seven years of proprietary interactive-lesson infrastructure plus a verifiable learning-reward dataset that frontier models lack, and argues consumer pricing benchmarked to human tutors (not games) is the right model. Jason Calacanis closes with a monologue on VC fundraising norms, deal-structure standards, and his own firm's first-meeting process.

insights.claim: Brilliant launched its AI tutor as a learning product on a Friday at the start of summer break and it went viral with almost 5 million views on X, which the CEO did not expect.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Demonstrates strong organic demand and distribution upside for consumer AI-learning products despite broad AI backlash.
insights.quote: this thing had almost 5 million views on X. We had no expectation that it would go as viral as it did.

insights.claim: Consumers are not anti-AI broadly but anti-job-replacement, anti-IP-theft and anti-slop; a product framed as 'AI that makes you a better thinker' resonates strongly with parents worried their kids can't read or do math.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Reframes the consumer-AI opportunity: pro-human, augmentation-positioned AI products can win where 'AI does it for you' messaging draws backlash.
insights.quote: people aren't anti-AII. That is like a very broad brush to paint people with. You know people are anti-diocracy. They're anti-IP theft. They're anti- being replacing their jobs. They're anti-slop.

insights.claim: Brilliant teaches transferable problem-solving and 'systems thinking' rather than memorized procedures, which the CEO argues builds intuition for what AI can and can't solve.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Differentiates Brilliant from rote test-prep apps and ties its pedagogy directly to the AI-era skill premium.
insights.quote: the difference is knowledge versus problem solving. And what brilliant teaches is problem solving. Problem solving is a very transferable skill.

insights.claim: Recent commencement speakers praising AI were booed off the stage at three or four 2026 graduations, which Calacanis attributes to graduates being the first cohort forced to use AI in school without resolution for their future.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Signals real consumer/cultural resistance to AI that pro-human products like Brilliant can convert into a positioning advantage.
insights.quote: All the commencement speakers that are talking about AI are getting booed off the stage. You know, AI is immensely uncool and unpopular with consumers.

insights.claim: Brilliant's tutor is largely model-agnostic; its edge over frontier models is a verifiable reward signal for tutoring (whether the student actually understands), which the company has at scale and frontier labs lack.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: The defensible asset is a proprietary RL reward dataset, not the base model — a moat that does not erode as foundation models improve.
insights.quote: frontier models don't have verifiable reward signals for tutoring and we do

insights.claim: Frontier models' ability to tutor well has plateaued since roughly o1 — they follow instructions and use tools better, but the core job of diagnosing and fixing student misunderstandings has not improved.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Capability gaps in pedagogy create durable room for vertical AI players even as general model benchmarks rise.
insights.quote: their ability to tutor well hasn't improved much since like 01 ... this core job of tutoring which is diagnosing and fixing student misunderstandings that's plateaued

insights.claim: Brilliant has built an interactive 'canvas' infrastructure since 2019 (GPT-2 era) where every interactive canvas exposes an API that LLMs can read and write to, letting the tutor see and annotate the student's work graphically.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Seven years of pre-built infrastructure is a hard-to-copy moat; the AI was layered onto purpose-built scaffolding, not bolted on.
insights.quote: since 2019 literally since GPT2 we have been designing an interactive uh canvas infrastructure where every interactive canvas has an API that LLM can read and write to.

insights.claim: Training the tutor was a major manual effort: expert teachers for every concept instructed the model on which tool calls to make and common misconceptions, so little of the tutoring methodology came free from the LLM.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: High labor intensity per subject is the cost barrier protecting incumbents and explaining the slow subject-by-subject rollout.
insights.quote: for every concept, we had expert teachers in that concept essentially instruct the model on which tool calls to make, what the common misconceptions are

insights.claim: The CEO believes the self-improving, hyper-personalizing tutoring architecture built on a dense user model and learning graph will not emerge from a foundation-model company anytime soon and must be vertically purpose-built.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Counters the 'OpenAI will eat the application layer' fear for education-specific verticals.
insights.quote: the architecture to self-improve and hyperpersonalize in real time based on a dense user model and learning graph, uh, we don't believe that that's going to come out of a model company anytime soon.

insights.claim: The tutor (Cooji) is designed to remove visual scaffolding progressively until the student is in a test-like, help-free environment, deliberately withdrawing the AI once mastery is demonstrated.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Pedagogical design choice that counters 'AI does the work for you' criticism and supports the pro-thinking brand.
insights.quote: by the end, you're in like a, you know, test-like environment where it's just the problem and you have to do it without help. And then Koji disappears and you're on your own.

insights.claim: Brilliant gets multilingual tutoring 'for free' from foundation models, with localization described as astonishingly good, including using employee voices for tutor voices in languages like Korean.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Cheap, high-quality localization unlocks Brilliant's large international base (60% of users) without per-language manual cost.
insights.quote: The localization is astonishingly good ... this is the stuff that LLMs just shine at.

insights.claim: Brilliant chose direct-to-consumer over selling to schools because B2B education puts multiple intermediaries (district, teacher) between the company and the actual learner, degrading the feedback loop.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Argues consumer distribution yields a tighter learning loop and better product than the standard edtech B2B school-sales motion.
insights.quote: one of the problems with selling to schools is that you're pretty far from the actual learner

insights.claim: Selling to schools 'gives license' to build an uninspiring product because students are forced to use it, whereas a consumer product must be voluntarily engaging — Brilliant deliberately aligned incentives with learners.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Frames captive B2B distribution as a quality trap; investors can evaluate edtech engagement durability through this lens.
insights.quote: in a classroom, kids are forced to use the thing. And so I think it almost gives you license to build a product that h is not inspiring to use

insights.claim: Brilliant prices at roughly $20–$40 per month benchmarked to human tutors (cited at ~$10,000/year), not to apps or games, aiming to deliver most of a tutor's value at ~95% lower cost.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Pricing-to-substitute (tutors) rather than to category (apps) supports premium ARPU; the most common customer complaint is that it is too cheap, signaling pricing power.
insights.quote: the main pricing question from our customers is not why is this so expensive, it's why is this so cheap.

insights.claim: Human tutoring costs roughly $80/session in middle America and $150–$300 per 90-minute session in Silicon Valley/Austin, and effectiveness requires high-dosage tutoring (~three times a week), pricing most families out.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host) and Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Quantifies the affordability gap Brilliant's $30/month product is targeting, sizing the substitution opportunity.
insights.quote: I think it's $150 to $300 per like 90 minute session ... let's call it 80 bucks in middle America

insights.claim: App-store reviews, refund requests, trials, unsubscribes and emailed feature requests give scaled consumer apps a feedback density that B2B sales cannot match, and Brilliant has multiple humans reading every single app-store review.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Real-time granular consumer feedback is treated as a strategic product-roadmap asset and north star.
insights.quote: the app store reviews are gold. Uh we still have multiple humans reading every single app store review.

insights.claim: Brilliant has about 70 employees and does not publicly disclose revenue or membership numbers, though the CEO hinted major milestones may be announced soon.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Lean headcount relative to scale suggests high revenue-per-employee efficiency for a decade-old consumer company.
insights.quote: Like about 70.

insights.claim: Brilliant's vision is a tutor in every home, language and subject; it is near curriculum-complete in middle/high-school math, is tackling college-level material and foundational coding this year, and expanding into science next year.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Maps a multi-vertical expansion path; each new subject is a TAM expansion lever now compressible from years to months with AI.
insights.quote: We cover math and coding. Now we are getting very close to uh curriculum complete in math for middle school and high school and then we're tackling college level material.

insights.claim: Brilliant believes it holds a uniquely valuable dataset of real student tutoring sessions at scale and sees this as the 'ChatGPT moment for AI and learning', anticipating it will soon fine-tune models specifically for tutoring.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Frames Brilliant as a potential data/fine-tuning supplier to model labs, hinting at an additional business line beyond consumer subscriptions.
insights.quote: this is like the chat GBT moment for AI and learning. You know we are a week into this.

insights.claim: Brilliant originated as a pivot from All Tuition, a 'LendingTree for private student loans' that processed hundreds of millions in loan consolidations, after investor Chamath Palihapitiya argued saving people on rates would never fix student debt that 'shouldn't exist in the first place'.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: Illustrates a first-principles pivot from a profitable-but-misaligned business to a mission-aligned one, and Chamath/Calacanis's decade-plus cap-table involvement.
insights.quote: the problem isn't that the rates are expensive. The problem is that they shouldn't exist in the first place.

insights.claim: Calacanis argues established companies that deeply understand their customers should treat AI as 'jet fuel' to disrupt themselves and compress vertical build times, rather than fearing disruption.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Investment lens: incumbents with strong customer relationships and data may be better AI-era bets than the narrative of incumbents-getting-killed suggests.
insights.quote: AI is jet fuel ... just build a second booster, a third booster, whatever, and just embrace it fully ... you won't get disrupted. You'll be the disruptor.

insights.claim: AI forces a binary divide where it either makes users smarter and more active or passive and dumber, and Brilliant's CEO is betting the company on the 'better thinker' side of that split.
insights.speaker: Sue Kim, CEO Brilliant
insights.so_what: A positioning thesis useful for evaluating which consumer-AI products will retain users vs. erode their own value proposition.
insights.quote: AI clearly is going to either make you smarter or it's going to make you passive and dumb.

insights.claim: AI can compress the time to build out a new education vertical from about 10 years to roughly 10 months to 2 years, materially accelerating Brilliant's expansion economics.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host) and Sue Kim
insights.so_what: Faster vertical rollout shortens payback periods and raises the value of a proven AI-tutoring platform.
insights.quote: instead of it taking 10 years to build a vertical, it takes you two years ... Or 10 months.

insights.claim: Calacanis argues inexperienced VCs struggle with products that don't fit an existing TAM, citing that the TAM for Airbnb and ride-sharing was effectively zero because they induced new markets to exist.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Investors should weight market-creation potential over current TAM; category-defining bets look small on paper.
insights.quote: The TAM for Airbnb was basically zero. They induced a market to exist.

insights.claim: Calacanis advises founders to flag and avoid non-traditional deal structures, recommending standard terms: board observer at 5% ownership, a board seat above 10%, and pro-rata/super-pro-rata plus warrants for early risk-takers.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Concrete governance guardrails founders can use to keep cap tables and boards clean and financeable.
insights.quote: If somebody owns 5%, you can give them a board observer seat. If they over 10%, give them a board seat. You know, it's standard stuff.

insights.claim: A wave of 'VCs behaving badly' stories trended on X, including Greg Eisenberg's account of a GP sleeping for 30+ minutes during a $15M Series A pitch (9.7M views) and Travis Kalanick's 2001 story of pitching from a VC's parked Lexus.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Reflects recurring founder-VC power-dynamic tension that resurfaces every 5–10 years and shapes founder-friendly firm reputations.
insights.quote: I keep presenting my series A slides to an unconscious man in a Herman Miller chair

insights.claim: Calacanis's firm runs over 100 first meetings, auto-collects founder feedback scores days later, and restricts first calls to only its three highest-rated investors (stack-ranked), benching others until their scores improve.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A measurable, merit-based process for founder experience that other firms could adopt to improve deal-flow reputation.
insights.quote: first calls are only done by the three highest scored people ... they are the great three-point shooters on the team.

insights.claim: Calacanis ends every first meeting by asking to repeat the founder's vision back to them, a tactic he adopted after repeated feedback that investors 'didn't understand my business'.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: A cheap, replicable technique to signal attentiveness, surface omissions and correct misunderstandings in pitch meetings.
insights.quote: May I repeat your vision back to you so that I can make sure I understand it perfectly.

insights.claim: Calacanis claims VCs hired from established firms brought 'bad habits' — doing one meeting a day instead of three or four and assuming check-writing grants them dictation power — prompting him to build an associate-in-training program.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Signals a hiring thesis favoring trainable newcomers over experienced VCs who absorbed entitled power-dynamic norms.
insights.quote: they think that their power dynamic is that because they write a check ... they get to dictate these things. They don't.

insights.claim: Calacanis recounts that John Doerr attended his Mahalo.com pitch directly after an ER visit following a Woodside bike crash, framing it as the ultimate sign of investor commitment versus a rival firm (Mohr Davidow) that cancelled on him at the last minute.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis (host)
insights.so_what: Reputational contrast illustrating how investor reliability in the fundraising process compounds into decade-long goodwill or grudges.
insights.quote: John [__] door flipped his bicycle ... He made the meeting. He did not [sleep] off during it.
